【Day 26】And Just Like That, I Was Forced into Becoming a Frontend Engineer

The book version was essentially rewritten from scratch — I filled in the parts of the story I couldn't fully tell during the original IT Home serialization, and cleaned up sections where the writing wasn't precise enough. It's a version I'm much happier with.
If you enjoy this post and want more, or want the polished version, the book is the way to go. The blog and IT Home will keep the original serialized version for archive purposes — please treat the book as the authoritative reference.
And just like that, I was forced into becoming a frontend engineer
You read that right — due to company needs, I got dragged from PHP into being a frontend engineer.
Reason? Because I was the only one in the company who'd touched HTML & CSS. So my manager's brain logic went:
knows how to slice → knows frontend → must know JavaScript
So I got shoved onto the frontend path of no return XD.

Browsers use HTML to structure web content, CSS to style appearance, and JavaScript to add interactivity and dynamic effects. These three complement each other and together form the foundation of modern web pages.
Don't ask why I was so compliant. After touching HTML and CSS, it was like a new world opened up — I got really into frontend dev.
So when my manager pushed, I just fell into the pit and became a frontend engineer.
Daily office dialogue was something like:
"Ray, can you fix the company homepage?"
default to submitting the form. To avoid this, set the
The Weird Type Phenomenon with Button

"Ray, here's some JavaScript — can you take a look?"
One day, my manager dropped this on me.

My first internal reaction:
"Huh? I don't know JS either!" 🤯
But outwardly you have to fake professionalism, nodding "OK, I'll take a look." (Then secretly flipping through the manual XD.)
Luckily the code wasn't hard — basic DOM manipulation and event handling. I was relieved: "Phew, not too brutal — heaven smiled upon me XD."
This also led me to buy another course from the team that did
Build a Website with HTML and CSS
. That's when I really got to know HexSchool.
"JavaScript Intro - Apprentice's Trial" has since been replaced by a newer course. Consider checking
Not long after, my manager started prepping a new project and muttered to himself: "Maybe we'll try Vue.js for the next project."
My internal monologue: "Crap, I'm being forced to upgrade to new skills."
So I just went all-in and bought the HexSchool frontend bundle and started grinding.
And that's how I started my frontend engineer journey.
Compared to C, JavaScript is easier to learn and brings satisfaction faster. "Satisfaction" here means: paired with HTML and CSS, you can immediately see results on screen.
C, C++, etc. require more complex dev environments and more interaction through CLIs (Command Line Interfaces). For beginners, that can feel dry.
In the early years of the web, pages were very static. How static? Here's an early YouTube example.
Few visual elements — mostly text. Images were possible but it was still very static.
Pages were mostly structured with HTML and beautified with CSS. Looked better, but still lacked interactivity.
"Interactivity? You mean CSS animations?" you might think.

Here "interactivity" doesn't mean pretty visuals — it means users can interact with the page. Clicking buttons, filling forms, getting immediate response or feedback — like form validation or dynamic content updates.
CSS at the time could only provide visual effects, not true interactivity. The key was JavaScript — even called the "
For JavaScript, it glues HTML and CSS, making pages no longer just static but interactive.
Early on, JavaScript's main role was providing interaction. By now, its scope has expanded enormously — frontend, server-side, desktop apps, mobile apps — basically a fully general-purpose language.
We said JavaScript is relatively easy. How easy? Quick example:
language-js shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
// console.log outputs content to the console
The example above is actually PHP syntax translated to JavaScript. You can see the syntax structures are similar — that's part of why I ramped up on JavaScript quickly.
This post doesn't dive deep into JavaScript — not its focus.
If you're interested, see my earlier Ironman series: "
RE: Zero Starting Learning JS Life
JavaScript syntax is simple, but easily misunderstood — especially for beginners, due to syntax quirks. E.g.:

let a; // If a isn't assigned, its value is undefined
console.log(a); // undefined

let b = null; // null is a special value meaning "no value"
console.log(b); // null

console.log(a == b); // true
// If a isn't assigned, its value is undefined
// null is a special value meaning "no value"
Normally these two shouldn't be equal, but in JavaScript,
are treated as equal. The reason: JavaScript's type coercion and comparison operator behavior.
These quirks have earned JavaScript various titles:
The most misunderstood language in the world
So why is JavaScript still so popular?
I think mainly because there's a group of devs who love this language, continuously promoting it and fixing its flaws — making JavaScript better over time.
, leading to unclear variable scope and pollution. Later ECMAScript 6 (ES6) introduced
These improvements are why JavaScript holds its place in frontend dev and is now one of the most popular languages.
For deeper learning on JavaScript's features, consider my earlier series "
To really capture HTML / CSS / JavaScript's relationship:
the skeleton — provides structure and frame
the skin and clothing — provides appearance and aesthetic
the muscles and nervous system — provides movement and interaction
These are frontend dev's three pillars — none can be missing.
Eventually I genuinely fell in love with JavaScript's weird quirks. Lots of frustrating moments, but those very quirks are what made me get into the language.
